questions.q: What is the core idea behind distributional semantics, the theoretical foundation for word embeddings?
options: Words that appear in the same sentences must be synonyms
Words that appear in similar contexts tend to have similar meanings
The meaning of a word depends only on its spelling and length
Words must appear in the same paragraph to be related
questions.explanation: Distributional semantics is based on Harris's principle that 'words which are similar in meaning occur in similar contexts'. This principle underpins all modern word embeddings like Word2Vec and GloVe. Similar spelling or position alone don't determine meaning.

questions.q: How does Word2Vec differ in its two main architectures, Skip-gram and CBOW?
options: Skip-gram predicts context words from a target word, while CBOW predicts the target word from context words
Both architectures are identical in how they function
CBOW is faster but Skip-gram is only used for longer documents
Skip-gram requires labeled training data while CBOW does not
questions.explanation: Skip-gram (Skipgram) learns by predicting surrounding context from a center word, while Continuous Bag of Words (CBOW) predicts the center word from context. Skip-gram generally works better for rare words and larger datasets. Both use unsupervised training from raw text.

questions.q: What is cosine similarity and why is it the standard metric for comparing word embeddings?
options: It measures the Euclidean distance between two words in a document
It measures the angle between embedding vectors, ranging from -1 to 1, and is computationally efficient and semantically meaningful
It counts how many characters two words have in common
It calculates how frequently two words appear together
questions.explanation: Cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors, with values near 1 indicating similar words and near -1 indicating opposing words. It's invariant to vector magnitude and computationally efficient. Euclidean distance doesn't account for direction; character overlap is surface-level; co-occurrence frequency is different.

questions.q: What problem does the Word2Vec skip-gram architecture solve with negative sampling?
options: Negative sampling prevents the model from learning about rare words
Instead of updating all vocabulary words, it updates a small random sample of negative examples plus the true positive, making training efficient
Negative sampling ensures embeddings are always positive numbers
Negative sampling removes words with negative connotations from training
questions.explanation: Negative sampling approximates the expensive softmax loss by sampling only a few 'negative' words (random non-context words) along with the true positive, reducing computation from O(V) to O(k) where k is the sample size. This made Word2Vec practical at scale.

questions.q: What is a key difference between Word2Vec embeddings and GloVe embeddings?
options: Word2Vec uses a neural network while GloVe only works with small vocabularies
Word2Vec learns from local context windows while GloVe incorporates global co-occurrence matrix statistics
Word2Vec produces word vectors while GloVe produces sentence vectors
Both are identical and GloVe is just another name for Word2Vec
questions.explanation: Word2Vec learns from local context windows in prediction tasks. GloVe (Global Vectors) explicitly factorizes the global word co-occurrence matrix, combining local context with global statistics. GloVe generally handles frequent words better due to this global information.

questions.q: Why might a word embedding assign the vectors for 'king' and 'queen' directions that differ by approximately the same direction as 'man' and 'woman'?
options: This is purely coincidental and has no linguistic basis
Embeddings capture semantic relationships through vector arithmetic, where the gender direction emerges from training on natural language patterns
Only very large embedding models develop this property
Embeddings are explicitly trained to satisfy specific gender relationships
questions.explanation: The famous 'king - man + woman ≈ queen' relationship emerges naturally from distributional semantics because 'king' and 'man' appear in similar contexts, as do 'queen' and 'woman'. This reflects gender relationships present in the training data. It's not by accident nor explicit instruction.

questions.q: What is the embedding dimension (e.g., 300 in Word2Vec), and what is the trade-off in choosing this value?
options: It's the maximum number of words that can have embeddings
Higher dimensions capture finer semantic distinctions but require more data and computation; lower dimensions are simpler but may lose nuance
All embedding models must use exactly 300 dimensions
Dimension determines how many languages the embedding supports
questions.explanation: Embedding dimension is the size of the vector space. Typical values are 100-300 for traditional models. Higher dimensions provide more expressiveness but require more training data to avoid overfitting and use more memory. The optimal dimension depends on vocabulary size and available data.

questions.q: Why are contextual embeddings (like BERT embeddings) considered superior to static embeddings like Word2Vec for many tasks?
options: They are smaller in file size than Word2Vec
They assign different vectors to the same word depending on context, capturing polysemy and handling phrases better
Contextual embeddings require less training data
Static embeddings are actually superior and contextual embeddings are a marketing term
questions.explanation: Static embeddings like Word2Vec assign one vector per word regardless of context. Contextual embeddings like BERT generate different representations for the same word based on surrounding context, handling polysemy ('bank' as financial vs. river edge) and longer-range dependencies better.

questions.q: What does it mean when two words have a high cosine similarity in embedding space?
options: They must be synonyms with identical definitions
They tend to appear in similar contexts and likely share semantic or functional relationships
They appear on the same page in dictionaries
They have similar spelling or number of letters
questions.explanation: High cosine similarity indicates words appear in comparable contexts. This correlates with semantic similarity but can also capture functional or associative relationships. 'Dog' and 'bark' have high similarity but aren't synonyms. Spelling similarity is separate from semantic embeddings.

questions.q: What is the relationship between embedding vocabulary size and model quality in Word2Vec?
options: Larger vocabularies always produce better embeddings
Smaller vocabularies produce sharper embeddings but miss rare words; larger vocabularies require more training data to learn quality representations for all words
Word2Vec cannot handle vocabularies larger than 100,000 words
Vocabulary size has no effect on embedding quality
questions.explanation: Larger vocabularies increase the learning problem's difficulty—the model must learn quality representations for more words with fixed training data. This often leads to poor embeddings for rare words. Smaller vocabularies allow deeper learning of common words but lose coverage. Vocabulary size must be balanced with data.
